== VIAF cluster ID (P214) == * 251503828 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 9190005007154 == Commons category (P373) == * Niu-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '三重県多気郡多気町丹生3999番地', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Niu-jinja}} [[JA:丹生神社 (多気町)]] {{wikidata link|Q17229562}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2020年9月}} {{神社 | 名称=Niu Shrine | 画像=[[画像:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le haiden (La construction du culte).jpg|250px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}{{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}} Niu 3999 | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}}・{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and 16 other deities | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, former {{ill|lt=village shrine in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} | 創建={{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}} 16 ({{ill|lt=523|Q31642|WD=Q31642|1=523}}) | 札所等= | 例祭={{ill|lt=July 16|Q2731|WD=Q2731|1=July 16}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Niu Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Niu Shrine''' (にうじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Niu (Taki)|Q17213591|WD=Q17213591|lt=Niu|1=Nyū}}, {{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}, with {{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} as a principal deity. It is a historic shrine listed among the nine shrines of Iitaka District in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. === Origin === When {{ill|lt=Emperor Shōmu|Q349263|WD=Q349263|1=Emperor Shōmu}} built the Great Buddha Hall of {{ill|lt=Tōdai-ji|Q460367|WD=Q460367|1=Tōdai-ji}}, he prayed to the local deity for {{ill|lt=mercury|Q925|WD=Q925|1=Mercury (element)}} production, and mercury is said to have gushed forth immediately; thus it was named Niu Myōjin. Also, according to stories that rain came when {{ill|lt=Emperor Saga|Q316654|WD=Q316654|1=Emperor Saga}} prayed for rain and clear weather came when he prayed for clearing, Niu Daimyōjin is also called a deity of {{ill|lt=rain prayer|Q477738|WD=Q477738|lt=praying for rain|1=Rainmaking (ritual)}} and fair weather. In the medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Kitabatake clan|Q11403853|WD=Q11403853|1=Kitabatake clan}} of {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166|1=Ise Province}} worshiped here yearly and supported construction and repairs. Later, a letter of donation was issued by Furuta Daizen, lord of {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}}, donating 30 koku in {{ill|lt=1611|Q6768|WD=Q6768|1=1611}} ({{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 16). In the same year, Higaki Chikuzen, gon-negi of {{ill|lt=Toyouke Daijingū|Q11633343|WD=Q11633343|lt=Ise Outer Shrine|1=Toyōke Daijingū}}, also served as this shrine’s priest, and thereafter the Higaki line became priests (different from today). In {{ill|lt=1619|Q6804|WD=Q6804|1=1619}} ({{ill|lt=Genna|Q635245|WD=Q635245|1=Genna}} 5), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Yorinobu|Q1045763|WD=Q1045763|1=Tokugawa Yorinobu}} of the {{ill|lt=Kishū Domain|Q1069187|WD=Q1069187|1=Kishū Domain}} recognized 30 koku of {{ill|lt=shrine estate|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=shrine lands|1=Jisha-ryō}}. Later domain lords personally visited, prayed for state peace and abundant harvests, and donated gold and silver. In {{ill|lt=1776|Q6741|WD=Q6741|1=1605}} (An'ei 5), it came under direct control of the Kishū-domain temple-and-shrine magistrate and the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002|1=keidai}} became tax-exempt land. There were also periods when, from {{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631|1=1677}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 5), old shrine timber from {{ill|lt=Ise Jingū|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} after each {{ill|lt=sengū|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} was granted and used for rebuilding. On January 21, Meiji 41, more than ten precinct shrines were {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}}. Relics related to mercury are preserved together with nearby {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji Temple}}, suggesting a deep relation through mercury. === Precinct shrines and notable places === * Niu-Naka Shrine - deities: {{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|WD=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanayamahime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and 17 others One of the nine Iitaka District shrines in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō. It is recorded in sources such as old records of Ise Outer Shrine that much mercury was mined in surrounding mountains in ancient times and offered to the two Ise shrines; this shrine preserves sacred treasures such as mining hammers and channels. Also, from February 18 to April 18, Meiji 41, over 30 village shrines including Hachiman, Yahashira, Konpira, and Shiogaki Shrines were merged, forming the present arrangement. * Original Ise-tsubaki tree - The {{ill|lt=camellia|Q160121|WD=Q160121|1=Camellia japonica}} growing beside the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3080012|WD=Q3080012|1=Shinto architecture}} is traditionally said to be the {{ill|lt=original tree|Q11409660|WD=Q11409660|1=original tree}} of Ise-tsubaki (Taki Town designated natural monument No.1). === Precincts === ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le torii d'entrée.jpg|Entrance ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - La pierre "Megami".jpg|Goddess stone ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le Temple Shintô Nyûnaka-jinja.jpg|Niu-Naka Shrine ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le bois brut du camellia "Ise-tsubaki".jpg|Ise-tsubaki === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related items === {{commonscat|Niu-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Niu Daishi|1=Jingū-ji Temple}} (Niu-zan Jingū-ji) * {{ill|lt=Niu Mine|Q11368609|WD=Q11368609|1=Niu Mine}} {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:にうしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Niu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Niu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:三重県の旧村社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:多気町の歴史]] [[Category:多気町の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

== VIAF cluster ID (P214) == * 251503828 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 9190005007154 == Commons category (P373) == * Niu-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '三重県多気郡多気町丹生3999番地', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Niu-jinja}} [[JA:丹生神社 (多気町)]] {{wikidata link|Q17229562}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2020年9月}} {{神社 | 名称=Niu Shrine | 画像=[[画像:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le haiden (La construction du culte).jpg|250px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}{{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}} Niu 3999 | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}}・{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and 16 other deities | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, former {{ill|lt=village shrine in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} | 創建={{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}} 16 ({{ill|lt=523|Q31642|WD=Q31642|1=523}}) | 札所等= | 例祭={{ill|lt=July 16|Q2731|WD=Q2731|1=July 16}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Niu Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Niu Shrine''' (にうじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Niu (Taki)|Q17213591|WD=Q17213591|lt=Niu|1=Nyū}}, {{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}, with {{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} as a principal deity. It is a historic shrine listed among the nine shrines of Iitaka District in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. === Origin === When {{ill|lt=Emperor Shōmu|Q349263|WD=Q349263|1=Emperor Shōmu}} built the Great Buddha Hall of {{ill|lt=Tōdai-ji|Q460367|WD=Q460367|1=Tōdai-ji}}, he prayed to the local deity for {{ill|lt=mercury|Q925|WD=Q925|1=Mercury (element)}} production, and mercury is said to have gushed forth immediately; thus it was named Niu Myōjin. Also, according to stories that rain came when {{ill|lt=Emperor Saga|Q316654|WD=Q316654|1=Emperor Saga}} prayed for rain and clear weather came when he prayed for clearing, Niu Daimyōjin is also called a deity of {{ill|lt=rain prayer|Q477738|WD=Q477738|lt=praying for rain|1=Rainmaking (ritual)}} and fair weather. In the medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Kitabatake clan|Q11403853|WD=Q11403853|1=Kitabatake clan}} of {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166|1=Ise Province}} worshiped here yearly and supported construction and repairs. Later, a letter of donation was issued by Furuta Daizen, lord of {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}}, donating 30 koku in {{ill|lt=1611|Q6768|WD=Q6768|1=1611}} ({{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 16). In the same year, Higaki Chikuzen, gon-negi of {{ill|lt=Toyouke Daijingū|Q11633343|WD=Q11633343|lt=Ise Outer Shrine|1=Toyōke Daijingū}}, also served as this shrine’s priest, and thereafter the Higaki line became priests (different from today). In {{ill|lt=1619|Q6804|WD=Q6804|1=1619}} ({{ill|lt=Genna|Q635245|WD=Q635245|1=Genna}} 5), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Yorinobu|Q1045763|WD=Q1045763|1=Tokugawa Yorinobu}} of the {{ill|lt=Kishū Domain|Q1069187|WD=Q1069187|1=Kishū Domain}} recognized 30 koku of {{ill|lt=shrine estate|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=shrine lands|1=Jisha-ryō}}. Later domain lords personally visited, prayed for state peace and abundant harvests, and donated gold and silver. In {{ill|lt=1776|Q6741|WD=Q6741|1=1605}} (An'ei 5), it came under direct control of the Kishū-domain temple-and-shrine magistrate and the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002|1=keidai}} became tax-exempt land. There were also periods when, from {{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631|1=1677}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 5), old shrine timber from {{ill|lt=Ise Jingū|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} after each {{ill|lt=sengū|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} was granted and used for rebuilding. On January 21, Meiji 41, more than ten precinct shrines were {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}}. Relics related to mercury are preserved together with nearby {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji Temple}}, suggesting a deep relation through mercury. === Precinct shrines and notable places === * Niu-Naka Shrine - deities: {{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|WD=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanayamahime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and 17 others One of the nine Iitaka District shrines in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō. It is recorded in sources such as old records of Ise Outer Shrine that much mercury was mined in surrounding mountains in ancient times and offered to the two Ise shrines; this shrine preserves sacred treasures such as mining hammers and channels. Also, from February 18 to April 18, Meiji 41, over 30 village shrines including Hachiman, Yahashira, Konpira, and Shiogaki Shrines were merged, forming the present arrangement. * Original Ise-tsubaki tree - The {{ill|lt=camellia|Q160121|WD=Q160121|1=Camellia japonica}} growing beside the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3080012|WD=Q3080012|1=Shinto architecture}} is traditionally said to be the {{ill|lt=original tree|Q11409660|WD=Q11409660|1=original tree}} of Ise-tsubaki (Taki Town designated natural monument No.1). === Precincts === ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le torii d'entrée.jpg|Entrance ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - La pierre "Megami".jpg|Goddess stone ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le Temple Shintô Nyûnaka-jinja.jpg|Niu-Naka Shrine ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le bois brut du camellia "Ise-tsubaki".jpg|Ise-tsubaki === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related items === {{commonscat|Niu-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Niu Daishi|1=Jingū-ji Temple}} (Niu-zan Jingū-ji) * {{ill|lt=Niu Mine|Q11368609|WD=Q11368609|1=Niu Mine}} {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Niu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Niu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:三重県の旧村社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:多気町の歴史]] [[Category:多気町の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

== VIAF cluster ID (P214) == * 251503828 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 9190005007154 == Commons category (P373) == * Niu-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '三重県多気郡多気町丹生3999番地', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Niu-jinja}} [[JA:丹生神社 (多気町)]] {{wikidata link|Q17229562}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2020年9月}} {{神社 | 名称=Niu Shrine | 画像=[[画像:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le haiden (La construction du culte).jpg|250px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}{{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}} Niu 3999 | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}}・{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and 16 other deities | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, former {{ill|lt=village shrine in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} | 創建={{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}} 16 ({{ill|lt=523|Q31642|WD=Q31642|1=523}}) | 札所等= | 例祭={{ill|lt=July 16|Q2731|WD=Q2731|1=July 16}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Niu Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Niu Shrine''' (にうじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Niu (Taki)|Q17213591|WD=Q17213591|lt=Niu|1=Nyū}}, {{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}, with {{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} as a principal deity. It is a historic shrine listed among the nine shrines of Iitaka District in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. === Origin === When {{ill|lt=Emperor Shōmu|Q349263|WD=Q349263|1=Emperor Shōmu}} built the Great Buddha Hall of {{ill|lt=Tōdai-ji|Q460367|WD=Q460367|1=Tōdai-ji}}, he prayed to the local deity for {{ill|lt=mercury|Q925|WD=Q925|1=Mercury (element)}} production, and mercury is said to have gushed forth immediately; thus it was named Niu Myōjin. Also, according to stories that rain came when {{ill|lt=Emperor Saga|Q316654|WD=Q316654|1=Emperor Saga}} prayed for rain and clear weather came when he prayed for clearing, Niu Daimyōjin is also called a deity of {{ill|lt=rain prayer|Q477738|WD=Q477738|lt=praying for rain|1=Rainmaking (ritual)}} and fair weather. In the medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Kitabatake clan|Q11403853|WD=Q11403853|1=Kitabatake clan}} of {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166|1=Ise Province}} worshiped here yearly and supported construction and repairs. Later, a letter of donation was issued by Furuta Daizen, lord of {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}}, donating 30 koku in {{ill|lt=1611|Q6768|WD=Q6768|1=1611}} ({{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 16). In the same year, Higaki Chikuzen, gon-negi of {{ill|lt=Toyouke Daijingū|Q11633343|WD=Q11633343|lt=Ise Outer Shrine|1=Toyōke Daijingū}}, also served as this shrine’s priest, and thereafter the Higaki line became priests (different from today). In {{ill|lt=1619|Q6804|WD=Q6804|1=1619}} ({{ill|lt=Genna|Q635245|WD=Q635245|1=Genna}} 5), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Yorinobu|Q1045763|WD=Q1045763|1=Tokugawa Yorinobu}} of the {{ill|lt=Kishū Domain|Q1069187|WD=Q1069187|1=Kishū Domain}} recognized 30 koku of {{ill|lt=shrine estate|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=shrine lands|1=Jisha-ryō}}. Later domain lords personally visited, prayed for state peace and abundant harvests, and donated gold and silver. In {{ill|lt=1776|Q6741|WD=Q6741|1=1605}} (An'ei 5), it came under direct control of the Kishū-domain temple-and-shrine magistrate and the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002|1=keidai}} became tax-exempt land. There were also periods when, from {{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631|1=1677}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 5), old shrine timber from {{ill|lt=Ise Jingū|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} after each {{ill|lt=sengū|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} was granted and used for rebuilding. On January 21, Meiji 41, more than ten precinct shrines were {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}}. Relics related to mercury are preserved together with nearby {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji Temple}}, suggesting a deep relation through mercury. === Precinct shrines and notable places === * Niu-Naka Shrine - deities: {{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|WD=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanayamahime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and 17 others One of the nine Iitaka District shrines in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō. It is recorded in sources such as old records of Ise Outer Shrine that much mercury was mined in surrounding mountains in ancient times and offered to the two Ise shrines; this shrine preserves sacred treasures such as mining hammers and channels. Also, from February 18 to April 18, Meiji 41, over 30 village shrines including Hachiman, Yahashira, Konpira, and Shiogaki Shrines were merged, forming the present arrangement. * Original Ise-tsubaki tree - The {{ill|lt=camellia|Q160121|WD=Q160121|1=Camellia japonica}} growing beside the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3080012|WD=Q3080012|1=Shinto architecture}} is traditionally said to be the {{ill|lt=original tree|Q11409660|WD=Q11409660|1=original tree}} of Ise-tsubaki (Taki Town designated natural monument No.1). === Precincts === ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le torii d'entrée.jpg|Entrance ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - La pierre "Megami".jpg|Goddess stone ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le Temple Shintô Nyûnaka-jinja.jpg|Niu-Naka Shrine ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le bois brut du camellia "Ise-tsubaki".jpg|Ise-tsubaki === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related items === {{commonscat|Niu-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Niu Daishi|1=Jingū-ji Temple}} (Niu-zan Jingū-ji) * {{ill|lt=Niu Mine|Q11368609|WD=Q11368609|1=Niu Mine}} {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Niu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Niu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:三重県の旧村社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:多気町の歴史]] [[Category:多気町の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

== VIAF cluster ID (P214) == * 251503828 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 9190005007154 == Commons category (P373) == * Niu-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '三重県多気郡多気町丹生3999番地', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Niu-jinja}} [[JA:丹生神社 (多気町)]] {{wikidata link|Q17229562}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2020年9月}} {{神社 | 名称=Niu Shrine | 画像=[[画像:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le haiden (La construction du culte).jpg|250px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}{{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}} Niu 3999 | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}}・{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and 16 other deities | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, former {{ill|lt=village shrine in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} | 創建={{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}} 16 ({{ill|lt=523|Q31642|WD=Q31642|1=523}}) | 札所等= | 例祭={{ill|lt=July 16|Q2731|WD=Q2731|1=July 16}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Niu Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Niu Shrine''' (にうじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Niu (Taki)|Q17213591|WD=Q17213591|lt=Niu|1=Nyū}}, {{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}, with {{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} as a principal deity. It is a historic shrine listed among the nine shrines of Iitaka District in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. === Origin === When {{ill|lt=Emperor Shōmu|Q349263|WD=Q349263|1=Emperor Shōmu}} built the Great Buddha Hall of {{ill|lt=Tōdai-ji|Q460367|WD=Q460367|1=Tōdai-ji}}, he prayed to the local deity for {{ill|lt=mercury|Q925|WD=Q925|1=Mercury (element)}} production, and mercury is said to have gushed forth immediately; thus it was named Niu Myōjin. Also, according to stories that rain came when {{ill|lt=Emperor Saga|Q316654|WD=Q316654|1=Emperor Saga}} prayed for rain and clear weather came when he prayed for clearing, Niu Daimyōjin is also called a deity of {{ill|lt=rain prayer|Q477738|WD=Q477738|lt=praying for rain|1=Rainmaking (ritual)}} and fair weather. In the medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Kitabatake clan|Q11403853|WD=Q11403853|1=Kitabatake clan}} of {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166|1=Ise Province}} worshiped here yearly and supported construction and repairs. Later, a letter of donation was issued by Furuta Daizen, lord of {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}}, donating 30 koku in {{ill|lt=1611|Q6768|WD=Q6768|1=1611}} ({{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 16). In the same year, Higaki Chikuzen, gon-negi of {{ill|lt=Toyouke Daijingū|Q11633343|WD=Q11633343|lt=Ise Outer Shrine|1=Toyōke Daijingū}}, also served as this shrine’s priest, and thereafter the Higaki line became priests (different from today). In {{ill|lt=1619|Q6804|WD=Q6804|1=1619}} ({{ill|lt=Genna|Q635245|WD=Q635245|1=Genna}} 5), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Yorinobu|Q1045763|WD=Q1045763|1=Tokugawa Yorinobu}} of the {{ill|lt=Kishū Domain|Q1069187|WD=Q1069187|1=Kishū Domain}} recognized 30 koku of {{ill|lt=shrine estate|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=shrine lands|1=Jisha-ryō}}. Later domain lords personally visited, prayed for state peace and abundant harvests, and donated gold and silver. In {{ill|lt=1776|Q6741|WD=Q6741|1=1605}} (An'ei 5), it came under direct control of the Kishū-domain temple-and-shrine magistrate and the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002|1=keidai}} became tax-exempt land. There were also periods when, from {{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631|1=1677}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 5), old shrine timber from {{ill|lt=Ise Jingū|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} after each {{ill|lt=sengū|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} was granted and used for rebuilding. On January 21, Meiji 41, more than ten precinct shrines were {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}}. Relics related to mercury are preserved together with nearby {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji Temple}}, suggesting a deep relation through mercury. === Precinct shrines and notable places === * Niu-Naka Shrine - deities: {{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|WD=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanayamahime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and 17 others One of the nine Iitaka District shrines in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō. It is recorded in sources such as old records of Ise Outer Shrine that much mercury was mined in surrounding mountains in ancient times and offered to the two Ise shrines; this shrine preserves sacred treasures such as mining hammers and channels. Also, from February 18 to April 18, Meiji 41, over 30 village shrines including Hachiman, Yahashira, Konpira, and Shiogaki Shrines were merged, forming the present arrangement. * Original Ise-tsubaki tree - The {{ill|lt=camellia|Q160121|WD=Q160121|1=Camellia japonica}} growing beside the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3080012|WD=Q3080012|1=Shinto architecture}} is traditionally said to be the {{ill|lt=original tree|Q11409660|WD=Q11409660|1=original tree}} of Ise-tsubaki (Taki Town designated natural monument No.1). === Precincts === ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le torii d'entrée.jpg|Entrance ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - La pierre "Megami".jpg|Goddess stone ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le Temple Shintô Nyûnaka-jinja.jpg|Niu-Naka Shrine ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le bois brut du camellia "Ise-tsubaki".jpg|Ise-tsubaki === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related items === {{commonscat|Niu-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Niu Daishi|1=Jingū-ji Temple}} (Niu-zan Jingū-ji) * {{ill|lt=Niu Mine|Q11368609|WD=Q11368609|1=Niu Mine}} {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Niu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Niu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:多気町の歴史]] [[Category:多気町の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

== VIAF cluster ID (P214) == * 251503828 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 9190005007154 == Commons category (P373) == * Niu-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '三重県多気郡多気町丹生3999番地', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Niu-jinja}} [[JA:丹生神社 (多気町)]] {{wikidata link|Q17229562}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2020年9月}} {{神社 | 名称=Niu Shrine | 画像=[[画像:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le haiden (La construction du culte).jpg|250px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}{{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}} Niu 3999 | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}}・{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and 16 other deities | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, former {{ill|lt=village shrine in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} | 創建={{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}} 16 ({{ill|lt=523|Q31642|WD=Q31642|1=523}}) | 札所等= | 例祭={{ill|lt=July 16|Q2731|WD=Q2731|1=July 16}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Niu Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Niu Shrine''' (にうじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Niu (Taki)|Q17213591|WD=Q17213591|lt=Niu|1=Nyū}}, {{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}, with {{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} as a principal deity. It is a historic shrine listed among the nine shrines of Iitaka District in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. === Origin === When {{ill|lt=Emperor Shōmu|Q349263|WD=Q349263|1=Emperor Shōmu}} built the Great Buddha Hall of {{ill|lt=Tōdai-ji|Q460367|WD=Q460367|1=Tōdai-ji}}, he prayed to the local deity for {{ill|lt=mercury|Q925|WD=Q925|1=Mercury (element)}} production, and mercury is said to have gushed forth immediately; thus it was named Niu Myōjin. Also, according to stories that rain came when {{ill|lt=Emperor Saga|Q316654|WD=Q316654|1=Emperor Saga}} prayed for rain and clear weather came when he prayed for clearing, Niu Daimyōjin is also called a deity of {{ill|lt=rain prayer|Q477738|WD=Q477738|lt=praying for rain|1=Rainmaking (ritual)}} and fair weather. In the medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Kitabatake clan|Q11403853|WD=Q11403853|1=Kitabatake clan}} of {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166|1=Ise Province}} worshiped here yearly and supported construction and repairs. Later, a letter of donation was issued by Furuta Daizen, lord of {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}}, donating 30 koku in {{ill|lt=1611|Q6768|WD=Q6768|1=1611}} ({{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 16). In the same year, Higaki Chikuzen, gon-negi of {{ill|lt=Toyouke Daijingū|Q11633343|WD=Q11633343|lt=Ise Outer Shrine|1=Toyōke Daijingū}}, also served as this shrine’s priest, and thereafter the Higaki line became priests (different from today). In {{ill|lt=1619|Q6804|WD=Q6804|1=1619}} ({{ill|lt=Genna|Q635245|WD=Q635245|1=Genna}} 5), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Yorinobu|Q1045763|WD=Q1045763|1=Tokugawa Yorinobu}} of the {{ill|lt=Kishū Domain|Q1069187|WD=Q1069187|1=Kishū Domain}} recognized 30 koku of {{ill|lt=shrine estate|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=shrine lands|1=Jisha-ryō}}. Later domain lords personally visited, prayed for state peace and abundant harvests, and donated gold and silver. In {{ill|lt=1776|Q6741|WD=Q6741|1=1605}} (An'ei 5), it came under direct control of the Kishū-domain temple-and-shrine magistrate and the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002|1=keidai}} became tax-exempt land. There were also periods when, from {{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631|1=1677}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 5), old shrine timber from {{ill|lt=Ise Jingū|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} after each {{ill|lt=sengū|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} was granted and used for rebuilding. On January 21, Meiji 41, more than ten precinct shrines were {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}}. Relics related to mercury are preserved together with nearby {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji Temple}}, suggesting a deep relation through mercury. === Precinct shrines and notable places === * Niu-Naka Shrine - deities: {{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|WD=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanayamahime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and 17 others One of the nine Iitaka District shrines in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō. It is recorded in sources such as old records of Ise Outer Shrine that much mercury was mined in surrounding mountains in ancient times and offered to the two Ise shrines; this shrine preserves sacred treasures such as mining hammers and channels. Also, from February 18 to April 18, Meiji 41, over 30 village shrines including Hachiman, Yahashira, Konpira, and Shiogaki Shrines were merged, forming the present arrangement. * Original Ise-tsubaki tree - The {{ill|lt=camellia|Q160121|WD=Q160121|1=Camellia japonica}} growing beside the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3080012|WD=Q3080012|1=Shinto architecture}} is traditionally said to be the {{ill|lt=original tree|Q11409660|WD=Q11409660|1=original tree}} of Ise-tsubaki (Taki Town designated natural monument No.1). === Precincts === ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le torii d'entrée.jpg|Entrance ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - La pierre "Megami".jpg|Goddess stone ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le Temple Shintô Nyûnaka-jinja.jpg|Niu-Naka Shrine ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le bois brut du camellia "Ise-tsubaki".jpg|Ise-tsubaki === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related items === {{commonscat|Niu-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Niu Daishi|1=Jingū-ji Temple}} (Niu-zan Jingū-ji) * {{ill|lt=Niu Mine|Q11368609|WD=Q11368609|1=Niu Mine}} {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Niu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Niu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:多気町の歴史]] [[Category:多気町の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

== VIAF cluster ID (P214) == * 251503828 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 9190005007154 == Commons category (P373) == * Niu-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '三重県多気郡多気町丹生3999番地', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Niu-jinja}} [[JA:丹生神社 (多気町)]] {{wikidata link|Q17229562}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2020年9月}} {{神社 | 名称=Niu Shrine | 画像=[[画像:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le haiden (La construction du culte).jpg|250px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}{{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}} Niu 3999 | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}}・{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and 16 other deities | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, former {{ill|lt=village shrine in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} | 創建={{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}} 16 ({{ill|lt=523|Q31642|WD=Q31642|1=523}}) | 札所等= | 例祭={{ill|lt=July 16|Q2731|WD=Q2731|1=July 16}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Niu Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Niu Shrine''' (にうじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Niu (Taki)|Q17213591|WD=Q17213591|lt=Niu|1=Nyū}}, {{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}, with {{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} as a principal deity. It is a historic shrine listed among the nine shrines of Iitaka District in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. === Origin === When {{ill|lt=Emperor Shōmu|Q349263|WD=Q349263|1=Emperor Shōmu}} built the Great Buddha Hall of {{ill|lt=Tōdai-ji|Q460367|WD=Q460367|1=Tōdai-ji}}, he prayed to the local deity for {{ill|lt=mercury|Q925|WD=Q925|1=Mercury (element)}} production, and mercury is said to have gushed forth immediately; thus it was named Niu Myōjin. Also, according to stories that rain came when {{ill|lt=Emperor Saga|Q316654|WD=Q316654|1=Emperor Saga}} prayed for rain and clear weather came when he prayed for clearing, Niu Daimyōjin is also called a deity of {{ill|lt=rain prayer|Q477738|WD=Q477738|lt=praying for rain|1=Rainmaking (ritual)}} and fair weather. In the medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Kitabatake clan|Q11403853|WD=Q11403853|1=Kitabatake clan}} of {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166|1=Ise Province}} worshiped here yearly and supported construction and repairs. Later, a letter of donation was issued by Furuta Daizen, lord of {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}}, donating 30 koku in {{ill|lt=1611|Q6768|WD=Q6768|1=1611}} ({{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 16). In the same year, Higaki Chikuzen, gon-negi of {{ill|lt=Toyouke Daijingū|Q11633343|WD=Q11633343|lt=Ise Outer Shrine|1=Toyōke Daijingū}}, also served as this shrine’s priest, and thereafter the Higaki line became priests (different from today). In {{ill|lt=1619|Q6804|WD=Q6804|1=1619}} ({{ill|lt=Genna|Q635245|WD=Q635245|1=Genna}} 5), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Yorinobu|Q1045763|WD=Q1045763|1=Tokugawa Yorinobu}} of the {{ill|lt=Kishū Domain|Q1069187|WD=Q1069187|1=Kishū Domain}} recognized 30 koku of {{ill|lt=shrine estate|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=shrine lands|1=Jisha-ryō}}. Later domain lords personally visited, prayed for state peace and abundant harvests, and donated gold and silver. In {{ill|lt=1776|Q6741|WD=Q6741|1=1605}} (An'ei 5), it came under direct control of the Kishū-domain temple-and-shrine magistrate and the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002|1=keidai}} became tax-exempt land. There were also periods when, from {{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631|1=1677}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 5), old shrine timber from {{ill|lt=Ise Jingū|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} after each {{ill|lt=sengū|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} was granted and used for rebuilding. On January 21, Meiji 41, more than ten precinct shrines were {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}}. Relics related to mercury are preserved together with nearby {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji Temple}}, suggesting a deep relation through mercury. === Precinct shrines and notable places === * Niu-Naka Shrine - deities: {{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|WD=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanayamahime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and 17 others One of the nine Iitaka District shrines in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō. It is recorded in sources such as old records of Ise Outer Shrine that much mercury was mined in surrounding mountains in ancient times and offered to the two Ise shrines; this shrine preserves sacred treasures such as mining hammers and channels. Also, from February 18 to April 18, Meiji 41, over 30 village shrines including Hachiman, Yahashira, Konpira, and Shiogaki Shrines were merged, forming the present arrangement. * Original Ise-tsubaki tree - The {{ill|lt=camellia|Q160121|WD=Q160121|1=Camellia japonica}} growing beside the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3080012|WD=Q3080012|1=Shinto architecture}} is traditionally said to be the {{ill|lt=original tree|Q11409660|WD=Q11409660|1=original tree}} of Ise-tsubaki (Taki Town designated natural monument No.1). === Precincts === ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le torii d'entrée.jpg|Entrance ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - La pierre "Megami".jpg|Goddess stone ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le Temple Shintô Nyûnaka-jinja.jpg|Niu-Naka Shrine ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le bois brut du camellia "Ise-tsubaki".jpg|Ise-tsubaki === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related items === {{commonscat|Niu-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Niu Daishi|1=Jingū-ji Temple}} (Niu-zan Jingū-ji) * {{ill|lt=Niu Mine|Q11368609|WD=Q11368609|1=Niu Mine}} {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Niu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Niu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:多気町の歴史]] [[Category:Buildings in Taki Town]][[Category:qq]]

== VIAF cluster ID (P214) == * 251503828 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 9190005007154 == Commons category (P373) == * Niu-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '三重県多気郡多気町丹生3999番地', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Niu-jinja}} [[JA:丹生神社 (多気町)]] {{wikidata link|Q17229562}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2020年9月}} {{神社 | 名称=Niu Shrine | 画像=[[画像:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le haiden (La construction du culte).jpg|250px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}{{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}} Niu 3999 | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}}・{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and 16 other deities | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, former {{ill|lt=village shrine in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} | 創建={{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}} 16 ({{ill|lt=523|Q31642|WD=Q31642|1=523}}) | 札所等= | 例祭={{ill|lt=July 16|Q2731|WD=Q2731|1=July 16}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Niu Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Niu Shrine''' (にうじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Niu (Taki)|Q17213591|WD=Q17213591|lt=Niu|1=Nyū}}, {{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}, with {{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} as a principal deity. It is a historic shrine listed among the nine shrines of Iitaka District in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. === Origin === When {{ill|lt=Emperor Shōmu|Q349263|WD=Q349263|1=Emperor Shōmu}} built the Great Buddha Hall of {{ill|lt=Tōdai-ji|Q460367|WD=Q460367|1=Tōdai-ji}}, he prayed to the local deity for {{ill|lt=mercury|Q925|WD=Q925|1=Mercury (element)}} production, and mercury is said to have gushed forth immediately; thus it was named Niu Myōjin. Also, according to stories that rain came when {{ill|lt=Emperor Saga|Q316654|WD=Q316654|1=Emperor Saga}} prayed for rain and clear weather came when he prayed for clearing, Niu Daimyōjin is also called a deity of {{ill|lt=rain prayer|Q477738|WD=Q477738|lt=praying for rain|1=Rainmaking (ritual)}} and fair weather. In the medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Kitabatake clan|Q11403853|WD=Q11403853|1=Kitabatake clan}} of {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166|1=Ise Province}} worshiped here yearly and supported construction and repairs. Later, a letter of donation was issued by Furuta Daizen, lord of {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}}, donating 30 koku in {{ill|lt=1611|Q6768|WD=Q6768|1=1611}} ({{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 16). In the same year, Higaki Chikuzen, gon-negi of {{ill|lt=Toyouke Daijingū|Q11633343|WD=Q11633343|lt=Ise Outer Shrine|1=Toyōke Daijingū}}, also served as this shrine’s priest, and thereafter the Higaki line became priests (different from today). In {{ill|lt=1619|Q6804|WD=Q6804|1=1619}} ({{ill|lt=Genna|Q635245|WD=Q635245|1=Genna}} 5), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Yorinobu|Q1045763|WD=Q1045763|1=Tokugawa Yorinobu}} of the {{ill|lt=Kishū Domain|Q1069187|WD=Q1069187|1=Kishū Domain}} recognized 30 koku of {{ill|lt=shrine estate|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=shrine lands|1=Jisha-ryō}}. Later domain lords personally visited, prayed for state peace and abundant harvests, and donated gold and silver. In {{ill|lt=1776|Q6741|WD=Q6741|1=1605}} (An'ei 5), it came under direct control of the Kishū-domain temple-and-shrine magistrate and the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002|1=keidai}} became tax-exempt land. There were also periods when, from {{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631|1=1677}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 5), old shrine timber from {{ill|lt=Ise Jingū|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} after each {{ill|lt=sengū|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} was granted and used for rebuilding. On January 21, Meiji 41, more than ten precinct shrines were {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}}. Relics related to mercury are preserved together with nearby {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji Temple}}, suggesting a deep relation through mercury. === Precinct shrines and notable places === * Niu-Naka Shrine - deities: {{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|WD=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanayamahime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and 17 others One of the nine Iitaka District shrines in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō. It is recorded in sources such as old records of Ise Outer Shrine that much mercury was mined in surrounding mountains in ancient times and offered to the two Ise shrines; this shrine preserves sacred treasures such as mining hammers and channels. Also, from February 18 to April 18, Meiji 41, over 30 village shrines including Hachiman, Yahashira, Konpira, and Shiogaki Shrines were merged, forming the present arrangement. * Original Ise-tsubaki tree - The {{ill|lt=camellia|Q160121|WD=Q160121|1=Camellia japonica}} growing beside the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3080012|WD=Q3080012|1=Shinto architecture}} is traditionally said to be the {{ill|lt=original tree|Q11409660|WD=Q11409660|1=original tree}} of Ise-tsubaki (Taki Town designated natural monument No.1). === Precincts === ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le torii d'entrée.jpg|Entrance ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - La pierre "Megami".jpg|Goddess stone ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le Temple Shintô Nyûnaka-jinja.jpg|Niu-Naka Shrine ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le bois brut du camellia "Ise-tsubaki".jpg|Ise-tsubaki === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related items === {{commonscat|Niu-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Niu Daishi|1=Jingū-ji Temple}} (Niu-zan Jingū-ji) * {{ill|lt=Niu Mine|Q11368609|WD=Q11368609|1=Niu Mine}} {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Niu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Niu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Municipal History of Taki, Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Taki Town]][[Category:qq]]

'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Niu-jinja}} [[JA:丹生神社 (多気町)]] {{wikidata link|Q17229562}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2020年9月}} {{神社 | 名称=Niu Shrine | 画像=[[画像:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le haiden (La construction du culte).jpg|250px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}{{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}} Niu 3999 | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}}・{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and 16 other deities | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, former {{ill|lt=village shrine in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} | 創建={{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}} 16 ({{ill|lt=523|Q31642|WD=Q31642|1=523}}) | 札所等= | 例祭={{ill|lt=July 16|Q2731|WD=Q2731|1=July 16}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Niu Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Niu Shrine''' (にうじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Niu (Taki)|Q17213591|WD=Q17213591|lt=Niu|1=Nyū}}, {{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}, with {{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} as a principal deity. It is a historic shrine listed among the nine shrines of Iitaka District in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. === Origin === When {{ill|lt=Emperor Shōmu|Q349263|WD=Q349263|1=Emperor Shōmu}} built the Great Buddha Hall of {{ill|lt=Tōdai-ji|Q460367|WD=Q460367|1=Tōdai-ji}}, he prayed to the local deity for {{ill|lt=mercury|Q925|WD=Q925|1=Mercury (element)}} production, and mercury is said to have gushed forth immediately; thus it was named Niu Myōjin. Also, according to stories that rain came when {{ill|lt=Emperor Saga|Q316654|WD=Q316654|1=Emperor Saga}} prayed for rain and clear weather came when he prayed for clearing, Niu Daimyōjin is also called a deity of {{ill|lt=rain prayer|Q477738|WD=Q477738|lt=praying for rain|1=Rainmaking (ritual)}} and fair weather. In the medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Kitabatake clan|Q11403853|WD=Q11403853|1=Kitabatake clan}} of {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166|1=Ise Province}} worshiped here yearly and supported construction and repairs. Later, a letter of donation was issued by Furuta Daizen, lord of {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}}, donating 30 koku in {{ill|lt=1611|Q6768|WD=Q6768|1=1611}} ({{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 16). In the same year, Higaki Chikuzen, gon-negi of {{ill|lt=Toyouke Daijingū|Q11633343|WD=Q11633343|lt=Ise Outer Shrine|1=Toyōke Daijingū}}, also served as this shrine’s priest, and thereafter the Higaki line became priests (different from today). In {{ill|lt=1619|Q6804|WD=Q6804|1=1619}} ({{ill|lt=Genna|Q635245|WD=Q635245|1=Genna}} 5), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Yorinobu|Q1045763|WD=Q1045763|1=Tokugawa Yorinobu}} of the {{ill|lt=Kishū Domain|Q1069187|WD=Q1069187|1=Kishū Domain}} recognized 30 koku of {{ill|lt=shrine estate|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=shrine lands|1=Jisha-ryō}}. Later domain lords personally visited, prayed for state peace and abundant harvests, and donated gold and silver. In {{ill|lt=1776|Q6741|WD=Q6741|1=1605}} (An'ei 5), it came under direct control of the Kishū-domain temple-and-shrine magistrate and the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002|1=keidai}} became tax-exempt land. There were also periods when, from {{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631|1=1677}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 5), old shrine timber from {{ill|lt=Ise Jingū|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} after each {{ill|lt=sengū|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} was granted and used for rebuilding. On January 21, Meiji 41, more than ten precinct shrines were {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}}. Relics related to mercury are preserved together with nearby {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji Temple}}, suggesting a deep relation through mercury. === Precinct shrines and notable places === * Niu-Naka Shrine - deities: {{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|WD=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanayamahime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and 17 others One of the nine Iitaka District shrines in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō. It is recorded in sources such as old records of Ise Outer Shrine that much mercury was mined in surrounding mountains in ancient times and offered to the two Ise shrines; this shrine preserves sacred treasures such as mining hammers and channels. Also, from February 18 to April 18, Meiji 41, over 30 village shrines including Hachiman, Yahashira, Konpira, and Shiogaki Shrines were merged, forming the present arrangement. * Original Ise-tsubaki tree - The {{ill|lt=camellia|Q160121|WD=Q160121|1=Camellia japonica}} growing beside the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3080012|WD=Q3080012|1=Shinto architecture}} is traditionally said to be the {{ill|lt=original tree|Q11409660|WD=Q11409660|1=original tree}} of Ise-tsubaki (Taki Town designated natural monument No.1). === Precincts === ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le torii d'entrée.jpg|Entrance ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - La pierre "Megami".jpg|Goddess stone ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le Temple Shintô Nyûnaka-jinja.jpg|Niu-Naka Shrine ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le bois brut du camellia "Ise-tsubaki".jpg|Ise-tsubaki === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related items === {{commonscat|Niu-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Niu Daishi|1=Jingū-ji Temple}} (Niu-zan Jingū-ji) * {{ill|lt=Niu Mine|Q11368609|WD=Q11368609|1=Niu Mine}} {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Niu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Niu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Municipal History of Taki, Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Taki Town]]

== VIAF cluster ID (P214) == * 251503828 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 9190005007154 == Commons category (P373) == * Niu-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '三重県多気郡多気町丹生3999番地', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Niu-jinja}} [[JA:丹生神社 (多気町)]] {{wikidata link|Q17229562}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2020年9月}} {{神社 | 名称=Niu Shrine | 画像=[[画像:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le haiden (La construction du culte).jpg|250px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}{{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}} Niu 3999 | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}}・{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and 16 other deities | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, former {{ill|lt=village shrine in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} | 創建={{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}} 16 ({{ill|lt=523|Q31642|WD=Q31642|1=523}}) | 札所等= | 例祭={{ill|lt=July 16|Q2731|WD=Q2731|1=July 16}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Niu Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Niu Shrine''' (にうじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Niu (Taki)|Q17213591|WD=Q17213591|lt=Niu|1=Nyū}}, {{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}, with {{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} as a principal deity. It is a historic shrine listed among the nine shrines of Iitaka District in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. === Origin === When {{ill|lt=Emperor Shōmu|Q349263|WD=Q349263|1=Emperor Shōmu}} built the Great Buddha Hall of {{ill|lt=Tōdai-ji|Q460367|WD=Q460367|1=Tōdai-ji}}, he prayed to the local deity for {{ill|lt=mercury|Q925|WD=Q925|1=Mercury (element)}} production, and mercury is said to have gushed forth immediately; thus it was named Niu Myōjin. Also, according to stories that rain came when {{ill|lt=Emperor Saga|Q316654|WD=Q316654|1=Emperor Saga}} prayed for rain and clear weather came when he prayed for clearing, Niu Daimyōjin is also called a deity of {{ill|lt=rain prayer|Q477738|WD=Q477738|lt=praying for rain|1=Rainmaking (ritual)}} and fair weather. In the medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Kitabatake clan|Q11403853|WD=Q11403853|1=Kitabatake clan}} of {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166|1=Ise Province}} worshiped here yearly and supported construction and repairs. Later, a letter of donation was issued by Furuta Daizen, lord of {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}}, donating 30 koku in {{ill|lt=1611|Q6768|WD=Q6768|1=1611}} ({{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 16). In the same year, Higaki Chikuzen, gon-negi of {{ill|lt=Toyouke Daijingū|Q11633343|WD=Q11633343|lt=Ise Outer Shrine|1=Toyōke Daijingū}}, also served as this shrine’s priest, and thereafter the Higaki line became priests (different from today). In {{ill|lt=1619|Q6804|WD=Q6804|1=1619}} ({{ill|lt=Genna|Q635245|WD=Q635245|1=Genna}} 5), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Yorinobu|Q1045763|WD=Q1045763|1=Tokugawa Yorinobu}} of the {{ill|lt=Kishū Domain|Q1069187|WD=Q1069187|1=Kishū Domain}} recognized 30 koku of {{ill|lt=shrine estate|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=shrine lands|1=Jisha-ryō}}. Later domain lords personally visited, prayed for state peace and abundant harvests, and donated gold and silver. In {{ill|lt=1776|Q6741|WD=Q6741|1=1605}} (An'ei 5), it came under direct control of the Kishū-domain temple-and-shrine magistrate and the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002|1=keidai}} became tax-exempt land. There were also periods when, from {{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631|1=1677}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 5), old shrine timber from {{ill|lt=Ise Jingū|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} after each {{ill|lt=sengū|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} was granted and used for rebuilding. On January 21, Meiji 41, more than ten precinct shrines were {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}}. Relics related to mercury are preserved together with nearby {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji Temple}}, suggesting a deep relation through mercury. === Precinct shrines and notable places === * Niu-Naka Shrine - deities: {{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|WD=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanayamahime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and 17 others One of the nine Iitaka District shrines in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō. It is recorded in sources such as old records of Ise Outer Shrine that much mercury was mined in surrounding mountains in ancient times and offered to the two Ise shrines; this shrine preserves sacred treasures such as mining hammers and channels. Also, from February 18 to April 18, Meiji 41, over 30 village shrines including Hachiman, Yahashira, Konpira, and Shiogaki Shrines were merged, forming the present arrangement. * Original Ise-tsubaki tree - The {{ill|lt=camellia|Q160121|WD=Q160121|1=Camellia japonica}} growing beside the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3080012|WD=Q3080012|1=Shinto architecture}} is traditionally said to be the {{ill|lt=original tree|Q11409660|WD=Q11409660|1=original tree}} of Ise-tsubaki (Taki Town designated natural monument No.1). === Precincts === ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le torii d'entrée.jpg|Entrance ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - La pierre "Megami".jpg|Goddess stone ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le Temple Shintô Nyûnaka-jinja.jpg|Niu-Naka Shrine ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le bois brut du camellia "Ise-tsubaki".jpg|Ise-tsubaki === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related items === {{commonscat|Niu-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Niu Daishi|1=Jingū-ji Temple}} (Niu-zan Jingū-ji) * {{ill|lt=Niu Mine|Q11368609|WD=Q11368609|1=Niu Mine}} {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Niu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Niu Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Municipal History of Taki, Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Taki Town]]

== VIAF cluster ID (P214) == * 251503828 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 9190005007154 == Commons category (P373) == * Niu-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '三重県多気郡多気町丹生3999番地', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Niu-jinja}} [[JA:丹生神社 (多気町)]] {{wikidata link|Q17229562}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2020年9月}} {{神社 | 名称=Niu Shrine | 画像=[[画像:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le haiden (La construction du culte).jpg|250px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}{{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}} Niu 3999 | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}}・{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and 16 other deities | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, former {{ill|lt=village shrine in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} | 創建={{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}} 16 ({{ill|lt=523|Q31642|WD=Q31642|1=523}}) | 札所等= | 例祭={{ill|lt=July 16|Q2731|WD=Q2731|1=July 16}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Niu Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Niu Shrine''' (にうじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Niu (Taki)|Q17213591|WD=Q17213591|lt=Niu|1=Nyū}}, {{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}, with {{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} as a principal deity. It is a historic shrine listed among the nine shrines of Iitaka District in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. === Origin === When {{ill|lt=Emperor Shōmu|Q349263|WD=Q349263|1=Emperor Shōmu}} built the Great Buddha Hall of {{ill|lt=Tōdai-ji|Q460367|WD=Q460367|1=Tōdai-ji}}, he prayed to the local deity for {{ill|lt=mercury|Q925|WD=Q925|1=Mercury (element)}} production, and mercury is said to have gushed forth immediately; thus it was named Niu Myōjin. Also, according to stories that rain came when {{ill|lt=Emperor Saga|Q316654|WD=Q316654|1=Emperor Saga}} prayed for rain and clear weather came when he prayed for clearing, Niu Daimyōjin is also called a deity of {{ill|lt=rain prayer|Q477738|WD=Q477738|lt=praying for rain|1=Rainmaking (ritual)}} and fair weather. In the medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Kitabatake clan|Q11403853|WD=Q11403853|1=Kitabatake clan}} of {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166|1=Ise Province}} worshiped here yearly and supported construction and repairs. Later, a letter of donation was issued by Furuta Daizen, lord of {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}}, donating 30 koku in {{ill|lt=1611|Q6768|WD=Q6768|1=1611}} ({{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 16). In the same year, Higaki Chikuzen, gon-negi of {{ill|lt=Toyouke Daijingū|Q11633343|WD=Q11633343|lt=Ise Outer Shrine|1=Toyōke Daijingū}}, also served as this shrine’s priest, and thereafter the Higaki line became priests (different from today). In {{ill|lt=1619|Q6804|WD=Q6804|1=1619}} ({{ill|lt=Genna|Q635245|WD=Q635245|1=Genna}} 5), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Yorinobu|Q1045763|WD=Q1045763|1=Tokugawa Yorinobu}} of the {{ill|lt=Kishū Domain|Q1069187|WD=Q1069187|1=Kishū Domain}} recognized 30 koku of {{ill|lt=shrine estate|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=shrine lands|1=Jisha-ryō}}. Later domain lords personally visited, prayed for state peace and abundant harvests, and donated gold and silver. In {{ill|lt=1776|Q6741|WD=Q6741|1=1605}} (An'ei 5), it came under direct control of the Kishū-domain temple-and-shrine magistrate and the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002|1=keidai}} became tax-exempt land. There were also periods when, from {{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631|1=1677}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 5), old shrine timber from {{ill|lt=Ise Jingū|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} after each {{ill|lt=sengū|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} was granted and used for rebuilding. On January 21, Meiji 41, more than ten precinct shrines were {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}}. Relics related to mercury are preserved together with nearby {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji Temple}}, suggesting a deep relation through mercury. === Precinct shrines and notable places === * Niu-Naka Shrine - deities: {{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|WD=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanayamahime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and 17 others One of the nine Iitaka District shrines in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō. It is recorded in sources such as old records of Ise Outer Shrine that much mercury was mined in surrounding mountains in ancient times and offered to the two Ise shrines; this shrine preserves sacred treasures such as mining hammers and channels. Also, from February 18 to April 18, Meiji 41, over 30 village shrines including Hachiman, Yahashira, Konpira, and Shiogaki Shrines were merged, forming the present arrangement. * Original Ise-tsubaki tree - The {{ill|lt=camellia|Q160121|WD=Q160121|1=Camellia japonica}} growing beside the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3080012|WD=Q3080012|1=Shinto architecture}} is traditionally said to be the {{ill|lt=original tree|Q11409660|WD=Q11409660|1=original tree}} of Ise-tsubaki (Taki Town designated natural monument No.1). === Precincts === ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le torii d'entrée.jpg|Entrance ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - La pierre "Megami".jpg|Goddess stone ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le Temple Shintô Nyûnaka-jinja.jpg|Niu-Naka Shrine ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le bois brut du camellia "Ise-tsubaki".jpg|Ise-tsubaki === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related items === {{commonscat|Niu-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Niu Daishi|1=Jingū-ji Temple}} (Niu-zan Jingū-ji) * {{ill|lt=Niu Mine|Q11368609|WD=Q11368609|1=Niu Mine}} {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Niu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Niu Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Municipal History of Taki, Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Taki Town]]

== VIAF cluster ID (P214) == * 251503828 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 9190005007154 == Commons category (P373) == * Niu-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '三重県多気郡多気町丹生3999番地', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Niu-jinja}} [[JA:丹生神社 (多気町)]] {{wikidata link|Q17229562}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=2020年9月}} {{神社 | 名称=Niu Shrine | 画像=[[画像:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le haiden (La construction du culte).jpg|250px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}{{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}} Niu 3999 | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}}・{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and 16 other deities | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, former {{ill|lt=village shrine in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} | 創建={{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}} 16 ({{ill|lt=523|Q31642|WD=Q31642|1=523}}) | 札所等= | 例祭={{ill|lt=July 16|Q2731|WD=Q2731|1=July 16}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Niu Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Niu Shrine''' (にうじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Niu (Taki)|Q17213591|WD=Q17213591|lt=Niu|1=Nyū}}, {{ill|lt=Taki|Q1203963|WD=Q1203963|1=Taki, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Taki District|Q1042360|WD=Q1042360|1=Taki District, Mie}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}, with {{ill|lt=Haniyasuhime|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} as a principal deity. It is a historic shrine listed among the nine shrines of Iitaka District in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. === Origin === When {{ill|lt=Emperor Shōmu|Q349263|WD=Q349263|1=Emperor Shōmu}} built the Great Buddha Hall of {{ill|lt=Tōdai-ji|Q460367|WD=Q460367|1=Tōdai-ji}}, he prayed to the local deity for {{ill|lt=mercury|Q925|WD=Q925|1=Mercury (element)}} production, and mercury is said to have gushed forth immediately; thus it was named Niu Myōjin. Also, according to stories that rain came when {{ill|lt=Emperor Saga|Q316654|WD=Q316654|1=Emperor Saga}} prayed for rain and clear weather came when he prayed for clearing, Niu Daimyōjin is also called a deity of {{ill|lt=rain prayer|Q477738|WD=Q477738|lt=praying for rain|1=Rainmaking (ritual)}} and fair weather. In the medieval period, the {{ill|lt=Kitabatake clan|Q11403853|WD=Q11403853|1=Kitabatake clan}} of {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166|1=Ise Province}} worshiped here yearly and supported construction and repairs. Later, a letter of donation was issued by Furuta Daizen, lord of {{ill|lt=Matsusaka Castle|Q1010226|WD=Q1010226|1=Matsusaka Castle}}, donating 30 koku in {{ill|lt=1611|Q6768|WD=Q6768|1=1611}} ({{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 16). In the same year, Higaki Chikuzen, gon-negi of {{ill|lt=Toyouke Daijingū|Q11633343|WD=Q11633343|lt=Ise Outer Shrine|1=Toyōke Daijingū}}, also served as this shrine’s priest, and thereafter the Higaki line became priests (different from today). In {{ill|lt=1619|Q6804|WD=Q6804|1=1619}} ({{ill|lt=Genna|Q635245|WD=Q635245|1=Genna}} 5), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Yorinobu|Q1045763|WD=Q1045763|1=Tokugawa Yorinobu}} of the {{ill|lt=Kishū Domain|Q1069187|WD=Q1069187|1=Kishū Domain}} recognized 30 koku of {{ill|lt=shrine estate|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=shrine lands|1=Jisha-ryō}}. Later domain lords personally visited, prayed for state peace and abundant harvests, and donated gold and silver. In {{ill|lt=1776|Q6741|WD=Q6741|1=1605}} (An'ei 5), it came under direct control of the Kishū-domain temple-and-shrine magistrate and the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002|1=keidai}} became tax-exempt land. There were also periods when, from {{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631|1=1677}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 5), old shrine timber from {{ill|lt=Ise Jingū|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} after each {{ill|lt=sengū|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} was granted and used for rebuilding. On January 21, Meiji 41, more than ten precinct shrines were {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}}. Relics related to mercury are preserved together with nearby {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji Temple}}, suggesting a deep relation through mercury. === Precinct shrines and notable places === * Niu-Naka Shrine - deities: {{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|WD=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Mikoto|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kanayamahime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and 17 others One of the nine Iitaka District shrines in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō. It is recorded in sources such as old records of Ise Outer Shrine that much mercury was mined in surrounding mountains in ancient times and offered to the two Ise shrines; this shrine preserves sacred treasures such as mining hammers and channels. Also, from February 18 to April 18, Meiji 41, over 30 village shrines including Hachiman, Yahashira, Konpira, and Shiogaki Shrines were merged, forming the present arrangement. * Original Ise-tsubaki tree - The {{ill|lt=camellia|Q160121|WD=Q160121|1=Camellia japonica}} growing beside the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3080012|WD=Q3080012|1=Shinto architecture}} is traditionally said to be the {{ill|lt=original tree|Q11409660|WD=Q11409660|1=original tree}} of Ise-tsubaki (Taki Town designated natural monument No.1). === Precincts === ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le torii d'entrée.jpg|Entrance ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - La pierre "Megami".jpg|Goddess stone ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le Temple Shintô Nyûnaka-jinja.jpg|Niu-Naka Shrine ファイル:Le Temple Shintô Niu-jinja - Le bois brut du camellia "Ise-tsubaki".jpg|Ise-tsubaki === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related items === {{commonscat|Niu-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=Jingū-ji (Taki, Mie)|Q11589471|WD=Q11589471|lt=Niu Daishi|1=Jingū-ji Temple}} (Niu-zan Jingū-ji) * {{ill|lt=Niu Mine|Q11368609|WD=Q11368609|1=Niu Mine}} {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Niu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Niu Shrine]][[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:Municipal History of Taki, Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Taki Town]]
